title: Graph Neural Networks in the Wilson Loop Representation of Abelian Lattice Gauge Theories
abstract: Local gauge structures play a central role in a wide range of condensed matter systems and synthetic quantum platforms, where they emerge as effective descriptions of strongly correlated phases and engineered dynamics. We introduce a gauge-invariant graph neural network (GNN) architecture for Abelian lattice gauge models, in which symmetry is enforced explicitly through local gauge-invariant inputs, such as Wilson loops, and preserved throughout message passing, eliminating redundant gauge degrees of freedom while retaining expressive power. We benchmark the approach on both $\mathbb{Z}_2$ and $\mathrm{U}(1)$ lattice gauge models, achieving accurate predictions of global observables and spatially resolved quantities despite the nonlocal correlations induced by gauge-matter coupling. We further demonstrate that the learned model serves as an efficient surrogate for semiclassical dynamics in $\mathrm{U}(1)$ quantum link models, enabling stable and scalable time evolution without repeated fermionic diagonalization, while faithfully reproducing both local dynamics and statistical correlations. These results establish gauge-invariant message passing as a compact and physically grounded framework for learning and simulating Abelian lattice gauge systems.
summary: The authors present a Graph Neural Network that operates directly on gauge-invariant Wilson loops to simulate Abelian lattice gauge theories. The model efficiently predicts both static properties and semiclassical dynamics, offering a powerful tool for studying complex gauge-matter interactions without the overhead of redundant gauge degrees of freedom.
main_problem: Developing an efficient machine learning framework for Abelian lattice gauge theories that handles gauge redundancy and captures nonlocal correlations induced by gauge-matter coupling.
main_result: The proposed gauge-invariant GNN accurately predicts local and global observables in Z2 and U(1) models and serves as a scalable surrogate for semiclassical dynamics, capturing statistical properties like autocorrelation functions.
method: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) architecture using gauge-invariant Wilson loop inputs and message passing, combined with automatic differentiation to reconstruct gauge-covariant forces for dynamics.
model_or_system: Abelian lattice gauge theories (Z2 and U(1)) and U(1) quantum link models on a square lattice, featuring gauge-matter coupling via a tight-binding Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Wilson loops (plaquette and winding), local fermion density, total energy, and autocorrelation functions.
important_parameters: Inverse temperature (beta), chemical potential (mu), kinetic scale (tau), electric coupling (g), and magnetic strength (K).
main_assumptions: The architecture is specifically tailored for Abelian theories and assumes the gauge-invariant sector can be captured by local and global Wilson loops; dynamical results are currently limited to the semiclassical regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the GNN architecture, showing the mapping from gauge configurations to Wilson loops and the message-passing process. Figure 2 contains heat maps comparing GNN predictions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a gauge-invariant GNN architecture, benchmarks its performance on static observables in Z2 and U(1) models, and then demonstrates its utility as a surrogate for semiclassical time evolution in quantum link models.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a scalable way to simulate the time evolution of gauge-matter systems, bypassing the computational bottleneck of repeated exact diagonalization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Gauge-invariant GNN architecture. (a) The input gauge configuration is first mapped to gauge-invariant variables given by the Wilson loops W□on elementary plaquettes, which are used to initialize vertex features Vi,α on the lattice graph. (b) The initial node features V (1) i,α are constructed from the local plaquette Wilson loops surrounding each site. (c) Features are

FIG. 3. Performance of the gauge-invariant GNN on a 20×20 lattice for the U(1) gauge model. Panels (a,b) display the en- ergy density at half filling, while (c,d) show the local fermion density at quarter filling. Results are presented as density heat maps comparing ML predictions to exact diagonaliza- tion; the left column (a,c) shows training data and the right column (b,d) test data. The test performance yields MSE 4.6165 × 10−7 and R2 = 0.9826 for energy density, and MSE 1.572 × 10−5 with R2 = 0.9747 for fermion density.

FIG. 2. Benchmark performance of the gauge-invariant GNN on a 20 × 20 lattice for the Z2 gauge model. Panels (a,b) show the energy density at half filling, and (c,d) the local fermion density at quarter filling. Heat maps compare ML predictions with exact diagonalization, with color indicating sample density; (a,c) correspond to training data and (b,d) to test data. The test accuracy reaches MSE 5.207 × 10−7 and R2 = 0.9912 for energy density, and MSE 2.245 × 10−5 with R2 = 0.9830 for fermion density.

FIG. 4. Benchmark of the gauge-invariant GNN for local force prediction in the U(1) gauge magnet. Panels (a) and (b) show density heat maps comparing ML-predicted and exact forces for the training and test sets. The strong diagonal concentration indicates excellent agreement, with R2 ≈0.998, MSE ∼2.45 × 10−3, and MAE ∼3.46 × 10−2, demonstrating accurate reconstruction of the effective force field governing the semiclassical dynamics.

FIG. 5. Comparison of semiclassical dynamics in the U(1) quantum link model (gauge magnet). The time evolution of the real part of a Wilson loop, Re[W□(t)], evaluated on a randomly selected elementary plaquette, is shown for sev- eral representative trajectories. Solid lines (ML) are obtained using forces predicted by the gauge-invariant GNN, while dashed lines (ED) correspond to forces computed from exact diagonalization. The ML-driven dynamics accurately repro- duce the short-time behavior and overall oscillatory structure, while small deviations accumulate at longer times, consistent with typical trajectory divergence in learned dynamical sys- tems.
